insights: Customer Support is the fastest-processing department with a median processing time of 42.0 days—18.4% faster than the overall median of 51.5 days—indicating a meaningful and consistent operational advantage.

Customer Support’s speed advantage is strongly associated with a distinct expense composition: 68.4% One-time expenses (vs 51.5% across other departments) and 73.7% of expenses exceeding $75k (vs 14.6%), yet higher-value expenses process faster *within* Customer Support—a counterintuitive pattern not observed elsewhere.

Geographic concentration in Africa accounts for nearly half (47.4%) of Customer Support’s volume but correlates with slower processing (42-day median), while its North America subset processes in just 12 days—revealing that location-based performance is highly context-dependent and not transferable via simple regional alignment.

Factor effects are inconsistent across departments: Recurring expenses process faster in 5 of 7 departments, yet One-time expenses drive speed in Customer Support and Sales—demonstrating that no single expense-type strategy reliably improves speed enterprise-wide.

Adopting Customer Support’s factor mix (e.g., shifting to more One-time or high-value expenses) would yield limited or negative impacts on other departments’ processing times, confirming that structural workflow differences—not compositional attributes—are the primary driver of speed variation.

The 22.5-day median gap between Customer Support (42.0 days) and Finance (64.5 days) represents a 34.9% improvement potential, but this gap cannot be closed by replicating surface-level characteristics—it signals a need to investigate department-specific process design, approval routing, or tooling rather than expense profile adjustments.
summary: Customer Support leads all departments with a 42.0-day median expense processing time—18.4% faster than the enterprise median—and exhibits a unique profile: heavy concentration in high-value ($75k+) and One-time expenses, alongside geographic skew toward Africa (though its North America subset is dramatically faster at 12 days). However, these factors do not generalize: Recurring expenses accelerate processing in most departments except Customer Support and Sales, and forcing similar expense compositions elsewhere would not replicate the speed benefit. The evidence confirms that department-specific workflows—not expense type, amount, or location per se—are the dominant drivers of processing efficiency. Therefore, cross-departmental improvement should prioritize deep-dive process mapping and workflow benchmarking within Customer Support—not factor redistribution—starting with its North America subunit and approval-handling practices.
